clarifyingQuestions: Is Dove's 3-point share loss driven by losing existing buyers to competitors, or by failing to recruit new Gen Z buyers as they enter the category?
Does Unilever's $60M annual Real Beauty budget cover all body wash advertising, or is there additional product/ingredient-focused media running alongside the brand platform?
What is Native's price positioning relative to Dove — is the share loss a value trade-down or a premium trade-up?
Has Dove tested any ingredient-focused or sustainability-led messaging with Gen Z, or has the entire brand voice been 'Real Beauty' for the past 20 years?

subIssues: Is 'Real Beauty' still resonating with Dove's core 35-55 consumer, and driving purchase retention?
Is 'Real Beauty' failing to recruit Gen Z buyers, and why specifically?
Is the campaign's message being delivered in the channels Gen Z actually uses?
Is Native winning on a different value proposition (ingredients, sustainability) rather than competing on body image messaging?
Are Dove's share losses concentrated among younger buyers who skew toward Native?
Can Dove address the Native threat within 'Real Beauty' or does it require a new platform?
Should Dove segment messaging — maintain 'Real Beauty' for 35-55, launch an ingredient-led platform for Gen Z?
Should Dove refresh 'Real Beauty' with Gen Z-relevant executions while keeping the platform?
Should Dove retire 'Real Beauty' and build an entirely new brand platform for the next decade?

stepByStep.title: Diagnose whether the campaign or the strategy is the problem
stepByStep.detail: Separate the question: is 'Real Beauty' failing (wrong message) or aging (right message for the wrong segment)? Data shows it still resonates at 81% on 'stands for something meaningful' — so the message is not wrong. But 'brand feels fresh' dropped 13 points, and Gen Z affinity is 38% lower than Millennials. The platform is correct; the targeting and execution are the problem.

stepByStep.title: Apply STP: is Dove trying to be all things to all women?
stepByStep.detail: A 20-year-old campaign that was built on Millennial and Gen X insights is now running to Gen Z with the same message. STP asks: who is the target, what do they want, and how is Dove positioned against that need? Gen Z wants ingredient transparency and sustainability — not a body image conversation. Real Beauty doesn't speak to that.

stepByStep.title: Analyze the Native threat through 3C's
stepByStep.detail: Native (8% share, 18% growth) is winning on a completely different value proposition: ingredient clarity ('no parabens, no sulfates'), DTC transparency, and sustainability ethos. This is not a Real Beauty vs. Real Beauty competition — it's an entirely different conversation. Dove cannot out-Native Native with Real Beauty messaging.

stepByStep.title: Build the strategic options and evaluate
stepByStep.detail: Three options: (1) Retire Real Beauty — highest risk, destroys equity with loyal 35-55 core; (2) Segment messaging — maintain Real Beauty for 35-55, launch an ingredient-transparency platform for Gen Z under the Dove masterbrand; (3) Refresh Real Beauty executions for Gen Z channels (TikTok, Instagram) while keeping the brand theme. Option 2 is most defensible: protect core equity while recruiting the next generation.

stepByStep.title: Recommend with investment allocation
stepByStep.detail: Recommend Option 2 with a 60/40 budget split: $36M maintaining Real Beauty for the core 35-55 segment, $24M launching an ingredient-transparency and sustainability platform for Gen Z. Measure Gen Z trial and consideration at 12 months. Do not retire Real Beauty — the equity score of 81% for 'stands for something meaningful' is too valuable to abandon.

keyTerms.definition: How long a campaign idea remains effective before it becomes either dated or so familiar it loses impact; 'Real Beauty' at 20 years is one of the longest-running brand platforms in CPG history

The difference in brand affinity between younger and older cohorts; Dove's 38% Gen Z affinity gap vs. Millennials signals a recruitment failure that compounds as older loyalists age out of the category
Brand positioning built on product formula transparency ('no parabens, sulfate-free, naturally derived') rather than emotional or social benefit; Native's primary competitive weapon against Dove
Running distinct messages to different consumer segments under the same masterbrand; the recommended strategy for Dove — Real Beauty for 35-55, ingredient-transparency for Gen Z
The overarching parent brand (Dove) under which different product lines or messaging platforms exist; maintaining masterbrand equity while evolving segment messaging is the core strategic challenge

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer clearly distinguishes between the campaign message failing and the campaign aging out of relevance with a new generation, proposes a concrete segmentation strategy, and does not simply say 'retire' or 'keep' without reasoning. A weak answer treats the 3-point share loss as proof the campaign has failed without diagnosing whether Gen Z recruitment or existing buyer defection is the driver.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I recommend retiring, refreshing, or extending, I want to separate two questions: is Real Beauty the wrong message, or is it the right message aging out of relevance with a new generation of buyers? Those have very different strategic responses. I'll use ad evaluation to diagnose the campaign's performance by segment, STP to understand whether Dove is trying to be all things to all women, and 3C's to understand the Native competitive threat specifically. Ready?

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Three questions. First: is Dove's 3-point share loss driven by existing buyers leaving, or by failing to recruit Gen Z as they enter the category? The distinction matters — retention and acquisition require different fixes. Second: has Dove tested any ingredient-focused messaging with Gen Z, or has the entire brand been 'Real Beauty' for 20 years? Third: is Native winning on price, ingredients, or both — because if it's a value trade-down, that's a different problem than a premium trade-up.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Is Real Beauty Failing or Aging?
sampleResponse.script: Let me start with the data. Dove's 'stands for something meaningful' score is still 81% — that is an exceptionally strong equity score for any brand after 20 years. The message is not broken. But 'brand feels fresh and current' dropped from 74% to 61% in four years, and Gen Z affinity is 38% below Millennials. That tells me Real Beauty is not a failed campaign — it's an aging campaign. It built profound equity with its original audience and is now failing to recruit the next generation. The campaign itself is correct; the targeting and channel execution are the problem.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — STP: Is Dove Targeting Everyone and Reaching No One?
sampleResponse.script: Here's the STP issue. A campaign built on 2004 insights about Millennial and Gen X women's relationship with beauty standards is now running on TikTok to Gen Z buyers as if nothing has changed. But Gen Z's relationship with beauty is different — they care about ingredient transparency, sustainability, and brand authenticity defined by supply chain ethics, not body image. Real Beauty speaks to 'your body is beautiful as it is.' Gen Z is asking 'what's actually in this bottle?' Those are different conversations, and Dove is answering the wrong question for the younger segment.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — 3C's: The Native Threat
sampleResponse.script: Native is at 8% share and growing 18% annually. That's a brand that will be at approximately 17% share within five years if the trajectory holds — which would match Dove today. But Native is not competing on Real Beauty's terms. Native is winning on ingredient lists: no parabens, no sulfates, naturally derived, cruelty-free. That's a completely different value proposition. Dove cannot defeat Native by running more Real Beauty campaigns. Dove needs an ingredient-transparency response — which means either a product reformulation story or a new communication platform.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Strategic Options
sampleResponse.script: Three options on the table. Option one: retire Real Beauty. High risk — Dove's 81% equity score on 'stands for something meaningful' is a 20-year asset you cannot recreate. Retiring it abandons the 35-55 core who still respond and still buy. I'd rule this out. Option two: refresh Real Beauty for Gen Z channels — run the same brand platform but with new executions, new talent, new formats built for TikTok and Instagram. Moderate effectiveness — Gen Z may still find the body-image conversation dated or performative regardless of format. Option three: segment the messaging. Maintain Real Beauty for the 35-55 core, invest in an ingredient-transparency and sustainability platform for Gen Z under the Dove masterbrand. Different messages, same brand. This is what I recommend.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My recommendation is Option Three: segment the brand messaging. Allocate the $60 million annual budget as 60-40 — $36 million maintaining Real Beauty for the 35-55 core consumer, and $24 million launching a new Gen Z platform built on ingredient transparency, sustainability credentials, and DTC-native formats. The Gen Z platform should explicitly address the Native threat: Dove's ingredients, Dove's supply chain, Dove's commitment to no harmful chemicals. Measure Gen Z trial and brand consideration at 12 months post-launch as the success gate. Do not retire Real Beauty — an 81% equity score for 'stands for something meaningful' is irreplaceable. Evolve the brand, don't abandon it.

title: Evaluating Nike's 'Dream Crazy' Colin Kaepernick Campaign
prompt: Nike launched its 'Dream Crazy' campaign featuring Colin Kaepernick in September 2018. Within 48 hours of launch, #BoycottNike was trending, with videos of consumers burning Nike products. Nike's stock declined 3% on the day of launch. However, Nike's digital sales increased 31% in the week following the launch. You are Nike's CMO presenting a 6-month campaign evaluation. How do you frame this?
context: Nike's sales in the six months following the campaign grew 10% YoY vs. 7% in the prior period. Nike's brand consideration among 15-24 year olds increased 5 points. Among consumers 45+, brand consideration declined 4 points. Nike's research shows the target of 'the next generation of athletes' (15-25 year olds) responded overwhelmingly positively. Earned media from the campaign was valued at $163M in the first week alone.
whyThisFramework: Ad Evaluation: reverse-engineer the message — 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything' positions Nike as a brand with conviction, aligning with Gen Z/young Millennial values. Was it delivered effectively? Boycotts from an unintended audience prove it cut through. STP is the second lens: the campaign was precisely targeted at youth athletes, and for that segment, it worked measurably.
keyConsiderations: Short-term stock decline and boycott noise are not campaign failure metrics — they are signal
The campaign succeeded for its intended target (15-25 year olds) and failed with non-targets (45+)
Earned media at $163M in week one far exceeds the paid media investment
The willingness to alienate some consumers to deeply connect with the target is the strategic insight
clarifyingQuestions: Is Nike's CMO presenting this as a success or is leadership genuinely uncertain — because the framing of this presentation requires knowing what narrative we're defending?
How much was the paid media investment for this campaign — because the $163M earned media comparison only makes sense with the paid budget as a reference point?
Is the 3% stock decline on launch day an isolated data point, or did Nike's stock recover and exceed pre-campaign levels within the 6-month evaluation window?
Should we isolate the Kaepernick campaign's effect on the 10% revenue growth, or is there product line momentum (Jordan Brand, new shoe launches) that is confounding the attribution?

subIssues: Was 'Believe in something even if it means sacrificing everything' the right message for Nike to own, given its brand heritage?
Did the message resonate with the 15-25 target in measurable ways (consideration, intent, purchase)?
Was Kaepernick the right messenger — credible, admired, and relevant to the target's values?
Was the boycott from 45+ consumers a predictable and acceptable cost of the campaign?
Did the stock decline reflect permanent value destruction or a temporary noise reaction?
Did Nike manage the controversy in a way that deepened target loyalty rather than retreating?
Did 6-month revenue growth of 10% exceed baseline trend, and what portion is attributable to the campaign?
Did the 31% digital sales spike in week one convert to sustained purchase behavior?
Is the consideration gain among 15-24 year olds a durable asset or a short-term spike?

stepByStep.title: Frame the evaluation lens correctly
stepByStep.detail: Open by establishing the right evaluation criteria: a campaign should be judged by its performance with the intended target, not its reception by non-targets. Nike's target was 15-25 year old athletes. All metrics should be anchored to that group. The boycott from 45+ consumers is not a campaign failure — it is evidence the campaign had sharp targeting.

stepByStep.title: Evaluate message credibility
stepByStep.detail: 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything.' This message required Nike to actually take a position — sacrificing short-term sales with some consumers. Nike's willingness to do so made the message credible. A brand that says 'believe' but doesn't show belief is performative. Nike showed belief, which is why the target trusted it.

stepByStep.title: Assess campaign delivery and earned media ROI
stepByStep.detail: $163M in earned media in week one alone. The controversy was the amplification. Without the boycott noise, the campaign would have received normal media coverage. The controversy — which Nike anticipated — turned a campaign into a cultural moment. This is a sophisticated use of polarization as earned media strategy.

stepByStep.title: Quantify business outcomes by segment
stepByStep.detail: Target segment (15-24): brand consideration up 5 points — a durable asset. Non-target (45+): consideration down 4 points — an acceptable cost, particularly since this segment has lower lifetime value and lower athletic product purchase frequency. 6-month revenue: 10% growth vs. 7% prior period baseline = 3 points of incremental growth. Digital spike of 31% in week one shows purchase intent was immediate.

stepByStep.title: Address the stock decline and close
stepByStep.detail: The 3% stock decline was a one-day market reaction to controversy noise — an efficient market overreacting to social media sentiment. Present the 6-month stock recovery data (Nike stock recovered and exceeded pre-campaign levels) as evidence that the market ultimately agreed with Nike's strategic judgment. Revenue and long-term brand equity tell the real story.

keyTerms.definition: The deliberate choice to take a position that will alienate some consumers in order to deeply connect with the intended target; Nike's Kaepernick campaign accepted 45+ consumer backlash to win deep loyalty among 15-25 year old athletes

The ratio of organic media coverage generated by a campaign to its paid media investment; $163M in earned media in week one vastly multiplied Nike's paid investment through controversy-driven coverage
The share of a target audience that would consider purchasing a brand; Nike's 5-point gain among 15-24 year olds and 4-point loss among 45+ shows precise targeting effectiveness

The tension between immediate sales/stock performance and durable brand equity; the Kaepernick campaign sacrificed short-term stock price for long-term positioning as the brand of the next generation of athletes

Applying Segmentation-Targeting-Positioning to assess whether a campaign served its intended segment; Nike's campaign was precisely targeted at 15-25 year olds and the metrics confirm it succeeded with exactly that group

interviewerExpectations: A strong answer immediately reframes the 'failure' narrative by anchoring evaluation to the intended target, correctly interprets the 45+ backlash as evidence of sharp targeting rather than a problem, and translates the business outcomes into a clear ROI case. A weak answer treats the stock decline and #BoycottNike as evidence of campaign failure without distinguishing target vs. non-target reactions.

sampleResponse.label: Opening & Framework Choice
sampleResponse.script: Before I present the 6-month results, I want to establish the right evaluation frame — because some of you will have walked in here thinking the boycott proves this was a mistake. It doesn't. A campaign should be judged by its performance with the intended target, not its reception by people who were never the target. Nike's target was the next generation of athletes: 15 to 25 year olds. Every metric I'm about to present is measured against that audience. The 45-and-over consumer reaction is a separate signal — and I'll address it, but it's not the evaluation criteria. I'll use the ad evaluation framework to assess message, delivery, and business outcomes, and I'll use STP to validate that the targeting was precise. Let's go.

sampleResponse.label: Clarifying Questions
sampleResponse.script: Two questions before I present. First — does leadership have the 6-month stock price data? The 3% launch-day decline is the metric the board fixates on, but I want to show the full recovery curve. Second — do we have the paid media investment figure, so I can calculate the earned media multiplier? $163 million in earned media in week one is the headline number, but it's even more powerful when set against the paid spend.

sampleResponse.label: Step 1 — Was the Message Right?
sampleResponse.script: Let's start with the message: 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything.' This is not a product campaign — it's a values campaign. The question is whether Nike had the credibility to own a values statement. I'd argue yes, for two reasons. First, Nike's entire brand DNA is built on the athlete who pushes past limits — Just Do It. Believing in something despite sacrifice is the athletic metaphor extended to civil life. Second — and this is the critical part — Nike actually demonstrated belief by running this campaign despite knowing it would cost them something. A brand that says 'believe' but never believes itself is hollow. Nike believed, and that made the message credible to the target.

sampleResponse.label: Step 2 — Delivery and Earned Media
sampleResponse.script: On delivery: $163 million in earned media in the first week alone. Let me put that in context. A typical media buy of that scale would cost Nike tens of millions in paid placements. The controversy — #BoycottNike, the burning products, the cable news coverage — didn't hurt delivery. It was the delivery. Nike anticipated that a polarizing position would generate polarizing coverage, and polarizing coverage is free media at scale. The burning shoes looked like destruction; to a 17-year-old athlete watching, it looked like adults who don't understand them proving that Nike does.

sampleResponse.label: Step 3 — Target Segment Results
sampleResponse.script: Results for the intended target: 15 to 24 year olds, brand consideration up 5 points. That is a significant, durable brand equity gain in the highest-value future buyer cohort. Digital sales up 31% in the week of launch — purchase intent was immediate and expressed at the register, not just in surveys. Six-month revenue growth of 10% year-over-year versus 7% in the prior period — that's 3 points of incremental growth, and at Nike's revenue scale, 3 points is worth hundreds of millions of dollars in absolute terms.

sampleResponse.label: Step 4 — Non-Target Reaction in Context
sampleResponse.script: Now the 45-plus consumer: brand consideration down 4 points. I want to be direct: this was expected, it was acceptable, and it is not a symmetric loss. The 45-plus consumer purchases athletic apparel at lower frequency and has lower long-term value to Nike's growth equation. The 15-24 year old is the buyer Nike needs to lock in for the next 30 years. Trading 4 points of consideration with the non-target for 5 points of consideration with the target is a net-positive exchange. If we had run a campaign that held flat with 45-plus and moved nothing with 15-24, that would have been the failure. As for the stock: the 3-percent launch-day decline was market noise — an overreaction to social media sentiment. The stock recovered within two weeks and exceeded pre-campaign levels within a month. The market's final verdict agrees with ours.

sampleResponse.label: Conclusion & Recommendation
sampleResponse.script: My six-month verdict: this campaign is a clear success by every metric that matters. Message credibility: earned. Delivery: $163M in earned media in week one. Target performance: 5-point consideration gain, 31% digital sales spike, 10% six-month revenue growth. The controversy was not collateral damage — it was the mechanism. Nike took a position, the market tested that position, and the target responded with their wallets. The recommendation going forward: do not walk this back, do not soften the brand voice, and do not chase the 45-plus consumer with follow-up campaigns that signal retreat. That would signal to the 15-24 year old that Nike's conviction was a marketing stunt. Stay the course, deepen the athlete partnership pipeline, and measure 24-month consideration trends to confirm the equity gain is compounding. This campaign will be in marketing case studies for the next 20 years — and not as a cautionary tale.
